g.text: Snake and Ladder Game (Annotated for Interview)
Trace: "Player-1 rolls dice, gets 4, moves to cell 4. Cell has a ladder (4->25). Player jumps to 25. Player-2's turn. First to reach 100 wins."
Board, Dice, and Players. Constructor calls
initializeGame() which sets up a 10x10 board
with 5 snakes and 4 ladders, creates 1 dice,
is a 2D grid of Cells (10x10 = 100 cells).
Constructor initializes grid, then randomly places
is a single square. Most cells have
is a unified model for both snakes
NOT separate classes. Both are just a Jump
with start and end. The direction (up/down)
is determined by comparing start vs end.
STEP 3 - How positions map to cells.
If start < end -> it's a ladder.
If start > end -> it's a snake.
Creates Jump object, attaches to Cell."
2. Push same player to back of deque
"[P1, P2] -> pop P1, push P1 -> [P2, P1]
Next turn: pop P2, push P2 -> [P1, P2]"
Add Player-3? Just push to deque.
"1. If newPosition > board size: return original
"Player-1 rolls 4, moves to cell 4. Cell has
Jump(start=4, end=25) — it's a ladder! Player jumps to 25.
Player-2's turn. Rolls 6, moves to cell 6. No jump. Player-1
again. Rolls 3, at 28. Cell has Jump(start=28, end=8) — snake!
Falls to 8. First player to reach cell 99 wins."
start < end = ladder, start > end = snake.
supports N players with no code change.
All composition, no inheritance. Clean and simple."
Game orchestrates: board, dice, players, game loop
Board is 10x10 grid of Cells. Cell may have a Jump
Loop: roll -> move -> jumpCheck -> update -> win check
jumpCheck: start < end = ladder, start > end = snake
: Snake and Ladder are the SAME class (Jump). No inheritance needed.
Direction determined by start vs end comparison.
